content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Swift 6.3 is here with an official Android SDK, the @c attribute for C interop, module selectors, and Swift Build. Here’s what actually matters for iOS devs.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Head over to your app shipped on Swift 6.2. It works fine. OK, picture the same codebase — faster builds, cleaner interop, less boilerplate — without changing a line.
That’s not a sales pitch. That is what Swift 6.3 delivers quietly, not announced at WWDC.
For example, Swift 6.3 (due to be released March 24, 2026) is not an exciting one-off rewrite. No keynote-type syntax revamp here. It’s not a big bang of a release rather steady improvements in compiler, standard library, package manager and tooling that yield a kind of 10% better developer experience.
Heading: Official Swift SDK for Android
Android support in Swift has existed as, over the past few years, a scrappy community project. Swift 6.3 changes that permanently.
You can now:

• Native Android applications fully written in Swift

• Add Android as a target directly in your Swift packages

• Bridging Swift to an existing Kotlin/Java codebase using Swift Java & Swift Java JNI Core
